meta.titleTemplate: Is Your Charcoal Shipment Insured? Cargo Protection
meta.descriptionTemplate: Marine cargo insurance and transit protection for coconut charcoal: what's covered, why self-heating fire is excluded, and how the cargo is protected on the voyage.

h1: Is your charcoal shipment insured? Cargo protection & marine insurance
heroSubtitle: Marine insurance covers the external risks of an ocean voyage — but not self-heating, which is prevented, not insured. Here is exactly what is covered, what is not, and how the cargo is protected in transit.

hero.ctaQuoteAria: Ask about cargo protection on WhatsApp

intro.p1: This page sits under the {{brand}} Logistics pillar. It answers the buyer's real question about a Class {{unClass}} cargo — "is my money safe?" — honestly: through prevention and the right insurance, not a false promise of coverage.

keyFacts.heading: Protection at a glance
keyFacts.definition: Who insures the cargo, what the cover includes, and what it excludes.
keyFacts.arrangedValue: The buyer (EXW / FOB / CFR — no seller cover)
keyFacts.thermalValue: Thermal blanket loaded in the container

insurance.body1: Honesty first: self-heating fire arising from the cargo's own nature is inherent vice, and standard marine cargo insurance does not cover it. That is not a gap to hide — it is the reason prevention matters. You cannot insure your way out of self-heating; you prevent it with the SP 978 controls and a documented compliance record.
insurance.coverageIntro: What marine cargo insurance does cover — the external perils of the voyage:
insurance.body2: Insurance is arranged and held by the buyer. The incoterms we offer — EXW, FOB, and CFR — include no seller-arranged cover, so the factory never insures the cargo. Confirm the scope, sum insured, and exclusions in your policy or certificate.
insurance.rulesLinkLabel: How the incoterm sets who insures
insurance.regulationLinkLabel: The SP 978 controls that prevent self-heating

condition.body: Over four to eight weeks at sea the cargo has to stay dry, intact, and stable. Sealed primary packaging plus container desiccant manage moisture (working with the SP 978 headspace), a thermal blanket buffers temperature swings, boxes are clean and dust-free before loading, and the master-box stacking protects against breakage. The physical specifications live on the packaging page.

safetyLoop.body: Put together: the SP 978 controls reduce the self-heating risk at source, the factory's compliance record backs that up, and marine insurance covers the external perils — water, theft, general average, vessel casualty, external fire. The cargo is protected against external risk by insurance, and against self-heating by prevention, not a policy. Saying so is the trust signal.

media.alt: Container desiccant and a thermal liner inside a loaded coconut charcoal container
media.caption: Moisture and thermal protection inside a loaded container.

items.a: Marine cargo insurance is arranged and held by the buyer — the incoterms we offer (EXW, FOB, CFR) include no seller-arranged cover. The policy covers the external perils of the voyage; confirm the scope and sum insured with your insurer.

items.q: Does the insurance cover self-heating fire?
items.a: No. Self-heating fire arising from the cargo's own nature is inherent vice and is excluded from standard marine cargo insurance. That is exactly why the SP 978 prevention controls matter — you cannot insure self-heating, you prevent it. Insurance covers wetting, theft, general average, vessel casualty, and external fire.

items.q: How is the charcoal protected from moisture and damage in transit?
items.a: Sealed primary packaging plus container desiccant manage moisture, a thermal blanket buffers temperature swings, boxes are clean before loading, and master-box stacking limits breakage. The exact desiccant and liner specifications are on the packaging page.

items.q: What happens if the container is damaged?
items.a: Damage from an external peril — water ingress, an accident, general average, vessel casualty, or external fire — is what the buyer's marine cargo policy is for. File the claim with the insurer against the policy or certificate; self-heating damage, being inherent vice, is not covered.
